metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan (e.g., a git-revert strategy or a two-phase expand-contract where old module paths remain importable for one release cycle) AND specify how module-qualified imports and importlib/string references — which re-exports cannot fix — will be remediated.
findings.finding: The plan's compat remedy (re-export shims in package __init__ files) does not cover the class of breakage it explicitly acknowledges: module-qualified attribute access (e.g., acli_subprocess._run_acli) and importlib/string references. Re-exports fix 'from x import y' and 'import x; x.y' at the package level but cannot transparently redirect dotted-module-path attribute lookups or string-based module names. No rollback plan or expand-contract sequence is provided for the cases where re-exports are insufficient.
findings.location: What section ('preserving import paths via package __init__ re-exports') vs. cascade_flag ('module-qualified imports like acli_subprocess._run_acli, importlib/string refs')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section: 'Rollback: the sub-packaging commit is a single atomic move; if post-merge breakage is detected, git revert <sha> restores all paths. For module-qualified imports and importlib/string refs, a pre-move grep/ast scan enumerates every such reference; each is updated in the same commit, verified by the sweep AC.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand AC3 to require a loader-aware migration strategy: update `_loader.py`'s `_PACKAGE_DIR` resolution and all `lazy_load(key, filename)` call sites' bare filenames alongside the `__init__` re-exports, so the importlib-by-path loading pattern and module-qualified access (`acli_subprocess._run_acli`) remain functional after modules move to sub-packages.
findings.finding: The plan's AC3 states 'Import paths preserved via package re-exports' as the preservation mechanism, but the codebase's `_loader.py` uses `_PACKAGE_DIR = Path(__file__).parent` to resolve bare filenames (e.g. `lazy_load('rebar_reconciler.adf', 'adf.py')`). After moving modules to `adapters/jira/`, these filename arguments break regardless of `__init__` re-exports — re-exports fix dotted-import names, not file-path resolution. Additionally, module-qualified access (`acli_subprocess._run_acli` called through the module object) and test fixtures pre-seeding exact `sys.modules` keys cannot be fixed by re-exports alone. The stated mechanism cannot achieve the stated goal for this codebase's import topology.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'Import paths preserved via package re-exports (module-qualified imports still resolve)' + cascade_flag section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3 or a new AC5: 'The `_loader.py` sibling-loader is updated so `_PACKAGE_DIR` (or a per-sub-package equivalent) resolves files after the move; all `lazy_load(key, filename)` call sites with bare filenames are updated to the new relative paths; and the sys.modules key contract (flat keys like `"rebar_reconciler.adf"` used by test fixtures) is explicitly addressed — either by preserving flat keys via `sys.modules` aliasing in the sub-package `__init__`, or by updating test fixtures as part of this plan.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a pre-move probe step: grep/ast-scan the codebase for `importlib.import_module`, `__import__`, and string-qualified module references inside `_engine/rebar_reconciler/`, enumerate them, and confirm each is either covered by a re-export or explicitly updated — before the sub-packaging move begins.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that '__init__ re-exports' will preserve all import paths after the ~55-module sub-packaging move, but the cascade_flag itself explicitly flags 'importlib/string refs' (e.g., module-qualified strings passed to importlib.import_module). Package-level __init__ re-exports cannot fix string-literal import paths; those references will silently break at runtime. No probe, grep-audit, or pre-move inventory step is planned to discover and remediate these dynamic references before the move is executed.
findings.location: cascade_flag section; AC line: 'Import paths preserved via package re-exports (module-qualified imports still resolve)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC or a sequenced step: 'Run `grep -rn "importlib.import_module\|__import__" _engine/rebar_reconciler/` and produce an inventory; update any string-literal module paths that __init__ re-exports cannot cover; include at least one integration test that imports each adapter via the new path string before merging the move.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate and address the out-of-package **file-path-based importlib consumers** (`mcp_server.py:141`, `bridge_fsck.py:65`, `tests/_engine_path.py:26`, and ~15 test-bootstrap patterns using `SCRIPTS_DIR / 'rebar_reconciler' / '<module>.py'`) in the cascade sweep — `__init__` re-exports alone will NOT fix these callers if any named module moves to a sub-directory.
findings.finding: The plan's consumer enumeration (via `cascade_flag`) is too abstract to be actionable: it does not name the specific out-of-package callers that use hardcoded file-path `importlib.util.spec_from_file_location` loads against flat `rebar_reconciler/<module>.py` paths — these callers will break even if package `__init__` re-exports are added, because `__init__` re-exports fix dotted-import resolution but NOT file-path-based loads.
findings.location: Scope section — `cascade_flag` / AC line 3 ('Import paths preserved via package re-exports')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a fourth item to the cascade_flag: 'absolute file-path loads via spec_from_file_location (mcp_server.py:mode.py, bridge_fsck.py:classify.py, tests/_engine_path.py:acli.py, and ~15 SCRIPTS_DIR/rebar_reconciler/<module>.py test bootstraps) must be updated to use the new path OR the affected modules must remain at the top-level rebar_reconciler/ for now.' Also add an AC line: 'All spec_from_file_location callers outside rebar_reconciler/ verified against new layout.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default resolution strategy for importlib/string-qualified module references in the Scope section (e.g., 'rewrite all importlib("rebar_reconciler.X") call-sites in-place to use the new path; do not rely on __init__ re-exports for these').
findings.finding: The cascade_flag notes importlib/string-qualified references that __init__ re-exports cannot shim, but the plan does not state how these must be handled — rewrite in-place, or something else — deferring a real decision to the executor.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the cascade_flag: 'importlib/string refs must be rewritten in-place to the new canonical path; __init__ re-exports cover only attribute-access imports and do not satisfy importlib("module_name") call-sites.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion explicitly verifying that the importer/dynamic-reference sweep was performed (e.g., 'All module-qualified imports and importlib/string references to moved modules are updated or confirmed no-op by the re-export layer').
findings.finding: The cascade_flag calls out 'broad importer + dynamic-reference sweep (module-qualified imports like acli_subprocess._run_acli, importlib/string refs)' as a required work item, but no acceptance criterion verifies that this sweep was performed and all such references updated. This is a described deliverable with no covering AC.
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: 'A sweep of module-qualified imports (e.g., acli_subprocess._run_acli) and importlib/string references across the codebase is completed and all affected call sites are updated or explicitly documented as resolved by re-exports.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit verification criterion for import-path preservation: specify a CI-runnable import smoke-test or automated sweep that validates module-qualified and `importlib`-style imports resolve correctly after the restructure, covering the edge cases called out in the cascade_flag.
findings.finding: AC3 asserts 'module-qualified imports still resolve' and the cascade_flag explicitly calls out `importlib`/string-qualified references as risk, but the plan specifies no verification mechanism (e.g., import smoke-test, CI import sweep, or an explicit grep-and-test criterion) to confirm import-path preservation after moving ~55 modules.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC3: 'Verified by an import smoke-test script (e.g., `python -c "import acli_subprocess; from acli_subprocess import _run_acli"` for each moved module) executed in CI, plus a grep audit confirming all `importlib.import_module` string references have been updated or re-exported.'

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in AC3 (or the ADR) the exact re-export mechanism (module-object binding via `import` vs. name binding via `from … import`) that preserves module-qualified access, and add a sweep/test requirement covering importlib/string-ref imports called out in the cascade_flag.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'import paths preserved via package re-exports (module-qualified imports still resolve)' without distinguishing between attribute re-exports (which do NOT make a submodule importable at its old path) and actual module-alias re-exports. Moving e.g. acli_subprocess to adapters/jira/acli_subprocess.py and adding `from .adapters.jira import acli_subprocess` to __init__.py does make `rebar_reconciler.acli_subprocess` work as a module reference — but only if the re-export is `import`-style (binding the module object), not a `from … import _run_acli` name pull. The cascade flag calls out 'module-qualified imports like acli_subprocess._run_acli' as a risk, yet AC3 commits to this being solved by 're-exports' without specifying the mechanism.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'Import paths preserved via package re-exports (module-qualified imports still resolve)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the ADR, document that the __init__.py re-export must use `from .adapters.jira import acli_subprocess` (module-object binding), not `from .adapters.jira.acli_subprocess import …`, and require a grep/AST sweep for importlib.import_module / getattr string refs as a pre-merge gate on the move PR.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expand the 'acli transport' citation in the O5 inventory and the ADR transport section to explicitly enumerate all 5 acli-cluster modules (acli.py, acli_cli_ops.py, acli_subprocess.py, acli_graph.py, acli_rest.py) including acli_rest.py's REST-direct Jira surface.
findings.finding: The plan describes 'the acli transport' as a single entity, but the actual transport layer is a 5-module cluster: acli.py (facade/AcliClient), acli_cli_ops.py (CLI command ops), acli_subprocess.py (subprocess floor), acli_graph.py (graph mixin), and acli_rest.py (REST/urlopen mixin). The plan's cascade_flag only names 'acli_subprocess._run_acli' as the representative module-qualified reference, but acli_rest.py contains its own direct Jira HTTP calls (via urlopen) with no subprocess. If the ADR maps 'the acli transport' as one module, acli_rest.py's REST-transport responsibilities may be mis-classified or missed in the vendor-layer boundary.
findings.location: Citations (O5): 'the acli transport'; cascade_flag: 'module-qualified imports like acli_subprocess._run_acli'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether ACs 2–4 (sub-packaging, re-exports, second-backend docs) are deliverables of THIS ticket or a follow-on ticket sequenced after function-extraction/git-adapter; split into two tickets or add an explicit qualifier to ACs 2–4 stating they are deferred.
findings.finding: The Scope section defers the code move ('Sequence the code move AFTER the reconciler function-extraction and git-adapter tickets') but ACs 2–4 describe concrete code-restructuring deliverables (sub-packages created, import paths preserved, second-backend documented) with no qualifier that they are future-ticket work, implying all four ACs are exit criteria for this ticket. An implementer reading Scope would defer the move; reading the ACs they would execute it — two contradictory directions.
findings.location: Scope section vs. Acceptance Criteria (AC2, AC3, AC4)
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) mark ACs 2–4 as 'out of scope until function-extraction and git-adapter tickets land' and create a follow-on ticket, or (b) remove the sequencing deferral from Scope and accept that this ticket does the full move now.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the module count in the S5 citation and cascade_flag from '55' to the verified count (58 as of the last directory scan) so the ADR inventory and sweep scope are accurate.
findings.finding: The plan asserts '55 flat .py files' and 'sub-packaging moves ~55 modules', but a directory listing of src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/ counts 58 .py files. The ADR's inventory and the cascade_flag scope estimate rest on an understated module count.
findings.location: Citations section (S5) and cascade_flag paragraph: '55 flat .py files' / 'sub-packaging moves ~55 modules'
findings.suggested_fix: Update 'S5 — 55 flat .py files' to '58 flat .py files' and 'sub-packaging moves ~55 modules' to '~58 modules'; regenerate the ADR inventory from the actual directory listing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for every AC: e.g. a required ADR file path + section headings check (`grep -E '##.*(Inventory|Neutral|Layout|Interface)' docs/adr/NNN-*.md | wc -l` → 4); a `find _engine/rebar_reconciler -type d | sort` snapshot matching the ADR layout; a `python -c 'from rebar_reconciler import acli_subprocess'`-style import smoke-test for AC3; and a doc-location grep for AC4.
findings.finding: No AC has a concrete proving command or check that produces evidence of success; all four criteria are stated as outcomes with no associated verification step, leaving 'done' ambiguous to a reviewer or CI gate.
findings.suggested_fix:
Append to each AC a parenthetical proving command:
- AC1: `grep -cE '##' docs/adr/NNN-reconciler-vendor-seam.md` returns ≥ 4 named sections.
- AC2: `find _engine/rebar_reconciler -type d | sort` matches the directory tree committed in the ADR.
- AC3: `python -m pytest tests/test_imports.py` (or a one-liner `python -c 'import rebar_reconciler.acli_subprocess; import rebar_reconciler.jira_fields'`) exits 0.
- AC4: `grep -r 'adapters/<name>/' docs/` or equivalent returns the relevant documentation file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC or task to audit existing developer-facing docs (CLAUDE.md, README, architecture notes) for references to the current flat `rebar_reconciler/` module names and update them to reflect the new sub-package layout as part of this ticket.
findings.finding: The plan calls for creating new sub-package paths and an ADR, but does not include a step to audit and update any existing developer-facing documentation (e.g., CLAUDE.md, README, architecture notes, or onboarding guides) that currently references the flat `rebar_reconciler/` module layout by name. After the move, those references will describe a structure that no longer exists, even though code imports are preserved by re-exports.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider splitting into two tickets: (1) ADR only (design decision artifact, independently reviewable/mergeable) and (2) sub-packaging execution gated on the merged ADR, so seam design can stabilize before the large code move begins.
findings.finding: The ticket bundles two independently valuable and separately releasable outcomes — (1) an ADR design document and (2) a ~55-module sub-packaging code restructure — with an explicit sequential gate between them ('DESIGN/ADR FIRST … THEN execute'), suggesting they should be separate tickets to allow ADR review and stabilization before the risky code move begins.
findings.location: ## What — 'DESIGN/ADR FIRST (do this now)… THEN execute the sub-packaging'; ## Acceptance Criteria AC1 vs AC2/AC3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that the 'second-backend interface sketch' in AC1/AC4 is constrained to a lightweight *narrative* description (e.g. 'a second backend must implement X, Y, Z operations') rather than a formal typed Protocol/ABC — deferring the Protocol definition until a concrete second backend is in scope, consistent with the plan's own 'Do NOT build a speculative full adapter abstraction' guardrail.
findings.finding: AC1 requires 'the interface a second backend would implement' as part of the ADR, and AC4 formalises 'implement adapters/<name>/ against the seam' as a done criterion — but no second backend exists, none is scheduled in this ticket, and `AcliClient` already documents its consumed interface informally (acli.py:220–226). Designing a formal vendor-neutral Protocol/ABC for a single concrete implementation violates Rule-of-Three (one backend = one call-site) and risks premature abstraction that must be maintained even if the second-backend initiative never lands or lands with different requirements.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 ('second-backend interface sketch') and AC4 ('implement adapters/<name>/ against the seam')
findings.suggested_fix: Reword AC1 to: 'second-backend interface sketch: a prose description of the operations a second backend must cover (issue CRUD, field mapping, transport), without a formal Protocol definition' and AC4 to: 'Adding a second backend is documented as: implement adapters/<name>/ providing the operations listed in the ADR sketch'. This keeps the design intent while explicitly prohibiting a typed Protocol that would have only one implementor.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the 'second-backend interface sketch' in AC1/AC4 against the Rule-of-Three (name the planned second backend and reference any prior art for ticket-system adapter interfaces, e.g. dbapi2 / Django DB backend / issue-sync OSS tools), or explicitly scope the ADR to 'seam boundary only, interface TBD when N≥2'.
findings.finding: The plan proposes committing to a 'second-backend interface sketch' in the ADR (AC1, AC4) before any second backend exists or is scoped. This is a textbook anti-repackaging / Rule-of-Three trigger: designing an abstraction for N=1 concrete implementations. The plan hedges with 'Do NOT build a speculative full adapter abstraction ahead of the ADR', but then asks the ADR to define 'the interface a second backend would implement' — which IS that speculative abstraction. No prior art (e.g. Django's database backend protocol, SQLAlchemy's dialect layer, or Python's own dbapi2) is referenced to ground the interface design, and the only candidate second backend is unnamed ('planned' multi-backend).
findings.location: What section: 'DESIGN/ADR FIRST … (d) the interface a second backend would implement'; AC1: 'second-backend interface sketch'; AC4: 'Adding a second backend is documented as implement adapters/<name>/ against the seam'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
